video_title: R+L=J: who are Jon Snow's parents?
claim: Jon Snow is not Eddard Stark's bastard son but is actually the son of Rhaegar Targaryen and Lyanna Stark, born at the Tower of Joy and raised by Ned as his own to protect him from Robert Baratheon.
sub_claims: If Rhaegar and Lyanna were secretly married, Jon has a claim to the Iron Throne stronger than Daenerys's; if they weren't married, he remains an illegitimate/bastard contender who could possibly be legitimized, and his Night's Watch vows plus the difficulty of proving his parentage further complicate any claim.

As both a Stark and a Targaryen, Jon may be the 'Song of Ice and Fire' personified -- possibly the Prince That Was Promised, Azor Ahai, or one of the three heads of the dragon -- combining Stark warging ability with Targaryen fire/dragon affinity.
asx_verdict: The video concludes that given the accumulated textual hints it seems very likely Jon Snow is the son of Rhaegar Targaryen and Lyanna Stark, while treating the throne-claim and prophecy implications as more speculative and unclear.
notes: Single theory (R+L=J) with two extension sub-claims (Iron Throne claim contingent on a secret Rhaegar-Lyanna marriage; 'Song of Ice and Fire'/prophecy significance) that the video hedges more heavily ('it's been suggested', 'unclear') than the core parentage claim. All evidence/quotes are book-sourced (AGOT Ned POV, ACOK Dany POV); no show-only beats. Transcript is one unbroken paragraph with no timestamps or explicit chapter citations, so book_hint values are inferred from the narration's context, not stated cites.

paraphrase: The theory reads Lyanna's death-bed described as 'a bed of blood' at the Tower of Joy as the blood of childbirth, meaning the baby born there is Rhaegar and Lyanna's son.
book_hint: AGOT, Ned's fever-dream/vision of the Tower of Joy

paraphrase: It is argued to be very out of character for the painfully honorable Ned Stark to have fathered a bastard child outside his marriage.
spoken_quote: dishonor ... [himself] and ... dishonor ... Catelyn, in the sight of gods and men

paraphrase: Unlike Robert, who would swear undying love to women and forget them by evening, Ned is not that kind of man, making it unlikely he broke his wedding vows to Catelyn by fathering Jon.
spoken_quote: swear undying love and forget them before evenfall

paraphrase: The text explicitly describes Ned as a man who keeps his vows, reinforcing that he would not have broken his marriage vows to father Jon.
At one point Ned refers to Jon as his 'blood' but is never shown calling him his son.
Ned internally lists his children as 'Robb and Sansa and Arya and Bran and Rickon'; Jon is mentioned in the very next line, but not as part of that list of his children.
spoken_quote: Robb and Sansa and Arya and Bran and Rickon

paraphrase: Jon is said to look like Arya, and Arya is said to look like Lyanna, suggesting Jon shares Lyanna's bloodline resemblance.

During a conversation with Robert about the Rebellion, Ned thinks he has 'lived his lies for fourteen years'; Jon is fourteen years old at the start of the first book, implying the lie concerns Jon's birth.
book_hint: AGOT, Ned POV, conversation with Robert about the Rebellion
spoken_quote: lived his lies for fourteen years

paraphrase: Jon is framed as Ned's lie, one that 'haunts him at night' but that he persists in keeping.
Ned's reasoning for keeping the secret is presented as: some secrets are safer kept hidden, too dangerous to share even with those you love and trust.
spoken_quote: Some secrets are safer kept hidden. Some secrets are too dangerous to share, even with those you love and trust.

paraphrase: When Robert orders the assassination of Daenerys and her unborn child, Ned refuses, damaging his friendship with Robert and risking his honor and life -- part of a pattern of protecting Targaryen children.
Ned also opposed the killing of Rhaegar's wife and children during the Sack of King's Landing, mirroring his later protection of Jon.

It seems odd that three Kingsguard were stationed at the Tower of Joy with Lyanna rather than protecting the dead prince's surviving siblings; the theory explains this as them guarding the heir apparent -- the prince's son, Jon.
In the House of the Undying in Qarth, Daenerys has a vision of a blue flower growing from a chink in a wall of ice, filling the air with sweetness.
book_hint: ACOK, Dany POV, House of the Undying
spoken_quote: A blue flower [growing] from a chink in a wall of ice, and [filling] the air with sweetness

paraphrase: Blue flowers, specifically blue winter roses, are consistently associated with Lyanna Stark throughout the books.
At the Tourney of Harrenhal, Rhaegar chose Lyanna as Queen of Love and Beauty over his own wife, crowning her with blue winter roses.
A storm of blue rose petals appears in Ned's dream about Lyanna.
The statue of Lyanna in the Winterfell crypt bears a garland of blue winter roses.
The consistent blue-rose/Lyanna imagery combined with the vision's 'wall of ice' strongly suggests something of Lyanna's is at the Wall -- most likely Jon.
It's been suggested that being both a Stark and a Targaryen makes Jon 'the Song of Ice and Fire' personified, implying he's very important to the story, though what that means exactly is unclear.
Stark blood is associated with powers like warging, and Targaryen blood with an affinity for dragons and fire; having both would make Jon capable of impressive abilities.
Jon might be the Prince That Was Promised, Azor Ahai, one of the three heads of the dragon, or another prophesied figure.
If Jon is Rhaegar's son, he would have a claim on the Iron Throne, arguably stronger than Daenerys's.
The throne claim assumes Rhaegar and Lyanna were secretly wed, which could have happened despite Rhaegar's existing marriage to Elia since polygamy has some precedent among Targaryens.
If Rhaegar and Lyanna weren't married, Jon would still be considered a bastard and only an illegitimate contender for the throne, though he could possibly be legitimized.
Jon's Night's Watch vows -- to hold no lands and wear no crown -- are a complication, since he has sworn them and so surely cannot be king.
spoken_quote: hold no lands ... wear no crown

paraphrase: Even if Jon could overcome the Night's Watch vow barrier, it's doubtful anyone would believe his claim to the throne, since he'd have no way to prove his parentage.
It seems doubtful that Jon would even want the Iron Throne.
Beyond politics and power, learning his true parentage would be personally significant for Jon, who has struggled with rejection from Catelyn, alienation as a bastard, and not knowing his mother.
